papers.title: The international problem of weasel words
papers.body_text: The international problem of weasel words Britain | More weaselly said than done The international problem of weasel words Two countries divided by a common language April 23rd 2026 The problem for the British soldiers was English understatement. And, admittedly, the thousands of Chinese soldiers attacking them. It was 1951, it was the Korean war and 29 Brigade had been asked to defend a hill. Soon they were low on men, ammunition and hope. Things, their brigadier told his American allies, were “a bit sticky”. In British army-speak that meant “critical”. The Americans heard: we’re fine. And left him to it. America and Britain are two nations divided by a common language. They are likely to be more divided than usual when Donald Trump—a man who gilds most parts of the Oval Office, wears $5,000 suits and who, on social media, has called himself “THE KING”—meets Charles III, an actual king, who gilds little, wears old, patched suits and once called the prospect of kingship “ghastly”. It seems highly likely something will be lost in translation. If only English speakers could agree on what phrases like “highly likely” actually mean. They are all, it turns out, divided by our common language, especially on words of “estimative probability”. Say “highly likely” and it is highly likely, says Adam Kucharski, a mathematician who studied the matter, that different people will hear it differently, with listeners assigning the event it describes a probability of anywhere from 45% to 100%. It is probable that other words are worse—like “probable” (for which a probability of 25% to 100% is assumed). Nationality matters. Americans, Professor Kucharski found, offer “more optimism”. But it is a realistic possibility (0% to 100%) that some phrases are almost meaningless. Native speakers seem unqualified to use qualifiers. Happily help is at hand. Such weasel words are so important in intelligence that organisations like Nato and the British government have calibrated them. Each now has a numerical value—and is used carefully. Thus when the king and THE KING meet there is only a remote chance (no more than 5%) that it will go wrong. It is almost certain (95%-plus) that, as long as they avoid tricky topics, like the joy of kingship, it will be fine. Probably. ■ For more expert analysis of the biggest stories in Britain, sign up to Blighty, our weekly subscriber-only newsletter. This article was downloaded by zlibrary from https://www.economist.com//britain/2026/04/23/the-international-problem-of-weasel-words

questions.stem: The problem for the British soldiers was English understatement. And, admittedly, the thousands of Chinese soldiers attacking them. It was 1951, it was the Korean war and 29 Brigade had been asked to _____ a hill.
questions.explanation: 根据上下文，英军在朝鲜战争中被要求守卫阵地，故选 defend。

questions.stem: Soon they were low on men, ammunition and hope. Things, their brigadier told his American allies, were “a bit sticky”. In British army-speak that meant “critical”. The Americans _____ : we’re fine.
questions.explanation: 根据语义，美国人听到的解释是“我们很好”，符合语境中因为语言习惯差异造成的误解。

questions.stem: America and Britain are two nations divided by a common language. They are likely to be more divided than usual when Donald Trump—a man who _____ most parts of the Oval Office, wears $5,000 suits...—meets Charles III.
questions.explanation: gild意为“镀金”，文中对比Trump的奢华与Charles的节俭，gilds对应原文。

questions.stem: It seems highly likely something will be lost in _____. If only English speakers could agree on what phrases like “highly likely” actually mean.
questions.explanation: 两人因语言习惯不同导致理解偏差，即“lost in translation”（翻译中丢失了原意）。

questions.stem: They are all, it turns out, divided by our common language, especially on words of “estimative probability”. Say “highly likely” and it is highly likely, says Adam Kucharski, a mathematician who _____ the matter, that different people will hear it differently.
questions.explanation: Adam Kucharski作为数学家是对该问题进行了研究，故选studied。

questions.stem: It is probable that other words are worse—like “probable” (for which a probability of 25% to 100% is _____).
questions.explanation: 括号内给出的数值是一个假定范围，assumed符合语境。

questions.stem: Nationality matters. Americans, Professor Kucharski found, offer “more _____”.
questions.explanation: 根据后文对比，美国人倾向于认为概率更高，故表现出乐观。

questions.stem: But it is a realistic possibility (0% to 100%) that some phrases are almost meaningless. Native speakers seem _____ to use qualifiers.
questions.explanation: 根据前文提到的歧义，母语者似乎不具备正确使用限定词的能力，即unqualified。

questions.stem: Happily help is at hand. Such weasel words are so important in intelligence that organisations like Nato and the British government have _____ them.
questions.explanation: 为了消除歧义，组织对这些词进行了“校准”，calibrated意为校准。

questions.stem: Each now has a numerical value—and is used carefully. Thus when the king and THE KING meet there is only a remote chance (no more than 5%) that it will go _____.
questions.explanation: 这里指会面出错的几率很低，故用wrong。

questions.stem: It is almost certain (95%-plus) that, as long as they avoid _____ topics, like the joy of kingship, it will be fine.
questions.explanation: kingship被描述为ghastly，属于敏感/棘手的话题，用tricky。

questions.stem: The problem for the British soldiers was English _____. And, admittedly, the thousands of Chinese soldiers attacking them.
questions.explanation: 文章开篇点出英国人的understatement导致了沟通问题。

questions.stem: Soon they were low on men, _____ and hope. Things, their brigadier told his American allies, were “a bit sticky”.
questions.explanation: 低弹药是战争语境的常见表述，文中明确提到ammunition。

questions.stem: And left him to it. America and Britain are two nations _____ by a common language.
questions.explanation: 题目多次重复“divided by a common language”，这是文章核心论点。

questions.stem: meets Charles III, an actual king, who gilds little, wears old, patched suits and once called the prospect of kingship “_____”.
questions.explanation: 对应原文Charles对王权的描述，ghastly意为可怕的。

questions.stem: If only English speakers could _____ on what phrases like “highly likely” actually mean.
questions.explanation: 即便是母语者也难以对词义达成一致，agree。

questions.stem: that different people will hear it differently, with listeners _____ the event it describes a probability of anywhere from 45% to 100%.
questions.explanation: 听者给事件赋予概率，assigning指赋予。

questions.stem: But it is a realistic possibility (0% to 100%) that some phrases are almost _____.
questions.explanation: 指某些词汇因歧义而变得没有实际意义，meaningless。

questions.stem: Such weasel words are so _____ in intelligence that organisations like Nato and the British government have calibrated them.
questions.explanation: 在情报工作中非常关键，故选important。

questions.stem: It is almost certain (95%-plus) that, as long as they avoid tricky topics, like the joy of kingship, it will be fine. _____.
questions.explanation: 结尾用Probably呼应前文提到的“概率”话题的讽刺意味。

papers.title: Four men deported by US to Eswatini have right to see lawyer, court rules
papers.body_text: Four men deported by the US to Eswatini and denied in-person legal counsel for nine months while detained in a maximum security prison have the right to see a local lawyer, Eswatini’s supreme court ruled. The men, from Cambodia, Cuba, Vietnam and Yemen, were sent to the small southern African country, formerly known as Swaziland, in July despite having no connection to the country, as part of Donald Trump’s administration’s efforts to ramp up deportations. The US government had labelled the men dangerous criminals. Their lawyers said they had already served sentences for crimes committed in the US. Eswatini’s correctional services refused to let a local lawyer see the men, although they allowed them to make calls to their US lawyers, the lawyers said. The court rejected the government’s argument that “the inmates persistently showed no interest” in meeting the human rights lawyer Sibusiso Magnificent Nhlabatsi, in a judgment handed down on Thursday. The three judges ruled: “There can be no real harm in granting the respondent access to the detainees … it then will be up to the detainees, if they do not wish to see the respondent, to tell this to the respondent to his face.” Of the first five deportees, one was repatriated to Jamaica in September. Another 10 arrived in Eswatini in October, one of whom was repatriated to Cambodia on 26 March, and a further four last month. Alma David, a US lawyer for several of the men, said: “The fact that it took nearly nine months of litigation and a decision by the highest court of the land to obtain something as innocuous as permission for my clients to meet with a local lawyer speaks volumes about how hard the government of Eswatini is fighting to deny these men the most basic of rights.” The Eswatini government spokesperson, Thabile Mdluli, did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Previously, she has said: “The government of the Kingdom of Eswatini has made every reasonable effort, in accordance with national laws and international obligations, to ensure that the third-country nationals received from the United States government are accommodated in conditions that respect their fundamental rights and human dignity.” The US has deported dozens of people to third countries from which they do not originate, including Ghana, South Sudan and Uganda. Human rights lawyers and NGOs have labelled the deportations as a form of human trafficking. A spokesperson for the US Department of Homeland Security said: “Allegations that sending criminal illegal aliens to third country’s [sic] is a form of ‘human trafficking’ is insane. The Trump administration is utilizing all lawful options to carry out the largest deportation operation in history, just as President Trump promised.”

questions.stem: What is the primary significance of the Eswatini supreme court’s recent ruling regarding the four men?

options.A: It mandates their immediate repatriation to their countries of origin.
options.B: It declares the US deportation policy as a violation of international human rights law.
options.C: It overturns the Eswatini government's decision to restrict the men's access to local legal counsel.
options.D: It forces the US government to provide financial compensation for the men's detention.

questions.explanation: 文章明确提到Eswatini最高法院裁定这些被拘留者有权见当地律师，并驳回了政府拒绝其会见律师的理由，因此选项C最符合文意。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the men’s status in Eswatini according to the text?

options.A: They were relocated to Eswatini as part of a voluntary rehabilitation program.
options.B: They have no prior ties to Eswatini, yet they are held in a maximum-security prison there.
options.C: They are considered citizens of Eswatini due to the US deportation agreements.
options.D: They have been granted the right to live freely in Eswatini after serving their sentences.

questions.explanation: 文中指出这些人被送往该国，但与该国没有联系（having no connection to the country），且被关押在最高安全级别监狱中，故选B。

questions.stem: How does Alma David characterize the government of Eswatini's handling of the case?

options.A: As a diligent attempt to uphold international justice and legal standards.
options.B: As a reluctant but lawful response to the pressures of the US administration.
options.C: As an obstructive effort to withhold even the most fundamental rights from her clients.
options.D: As a confused bureaucratic process due to the lack of clear national laws.

questions.explanation: Alma David认为政府花费九个月诉讼仅为了获得基本的会见许可，这表明政府在极力剥夺这些人的基本权利，故选C。

questions.stem: What is the stance of the US Department of Homeland Security regarding the criticisms labeled against their deportation policy?

options.A: They argue that the policy is a necessary and legal component of their immigration strategy.
options.B: They agree that human rights violations have occurred and promise an immediate review.
options.C: They acknowledge the term 'human trafficking' but argue it is justified in this context.
options.D: They remain silent on the matter and refuse to provide any official justification.

questions.explanation: 美国国土安全部发言人明确表示将关于“人口贩卖”的指控斥为“疯狂的”，并强调这是在执行“所有合法选项”，即认为其政策是合法且必要的，故选A。

questions.stem: What is the implication of the court's statement that there is 'no real harm' in granting access to detainees?

options.A: The court believes the lawyer represents a potential security risk to the prison.
options.B: The court views the government’s security concerns as overstated and insufficient to deny basic rights.
options.C: The court is implying that the detainees are not actually dangerous criminals.
options.D: The court intends to facilitate the future transfer of detainees to other countries.

questions.explanation: 法院认为允许律师进入不会造成实质性伤害，潜台词是政府以此作为拒绝理由是不成立的，即对政府安全担忧的否定，故选B。

papers.title: Young Chinese-Irish ballroom dancer goes international
papers.body_text: Kerry Sui and Francis Carty perform on the dance floor at Blackpool Dance Festival in November. [Photo provided to chinadaily.com.cn] Kerry Sui, a 10-year-old Chinese-Irish dancer, and her dance partner Francis Carty are set to compete in Italy and perform on Chinese TV after winning the British juvenile ballroom championship. Kerry and Francis, who is also 10, won the British National Juvenile Ballroom trophy and took first prize last month in the juvenile Latin category at the Blackpool Dance Festival. Kerry was born in Dublin and lives there with her parents, who emigrated from China. Her Chinese name is Sui Yuanjing. She started ballroom and Latin dancing four years ago and was partnered earlier this year with Francis, who lives in the English county of Shropshire. The two were friends before they performed together and Kerry said their friendship is one of the best things about her dancing partnership with Francis. Their hard work, under the guidance of teachers Laura O'Brien and David and Helen Truman, meant Kerry and Francis have seen quite a lot of success. "When Francis is stressed, I can calm him down, because he hasn't really been in many big competitions, such as the nationals and internationals," Kerry said. "My mum said I have to guide him." Francis said Kerry has a positive attitude and described her as always remaining upbeat. And they both have a dance that they like the least: the tango. "The tango is like an angry dance, you have to be sharp," Kerry said. The dancers often visit each other's families and describe their friendship and their teachers' involvement as like being in a big family. She said a visit to China put a strain on the close bonds. "I was in China for six weeks and there was a dance studio there where I would practice, but I felt lonely," Kerry said. Francis described the time they spent apart as "horrible". Kerry and Francis were training on Saturday in Wolverhampton, a city in England's West Midlands region, in advance of a competition in Stoke-on-Trent on Sunday. The event was cancelled due to the heavy snowfall across parts of the United Kingdom. The pair head to Italy in January for a competition, before jetting off to China to appear on a TV show.

questions.stem: According to the passage, what is the main reason for the success of Kerry and Francis?

options.A: They have lived in the same city since they were born.
options.B: They have received professional guidance and worked hard together.
options.C: They were born into families with a professional dancing background.
options.D: They have won numerous international trophies before the Blackpool festival.

questions.explanation: 文章提到“Their hard work, under the guidance of teachers... meant Kerry and Francis have seen quite a lot of success”，明确指出他们的成功源于勤奋以及教练的指导。

questions.stem: How does Kerry view her role in the partnership with Francis?

options.A: She sees herself as a mentor who must handle all the stress.
options.B: She believes she needs to be the one who calms him down and guides him.
options.C: She thinks she is the more talented dancer in the pair.
options.D: She feels that she must carry the burden of their shared success alone.

questions.explanation: 文中Kerry明确表示“When Francis is stressed, I can calm him down”以及“My mum said I have to guide him”，说明她认为自己有责任在比赛压力下引导和安抚舞伴。

questions.stem: What does the word "upbeat" most likely mean in the context of the passage?
questions.explanation: 文中Francis描述Kerry时提到“Kerry has a positive attitude and described her as always remaining upbeat”，根据语境positive（积极的）可以推断出upbeat意为乐观、开朗。

questions.stem: Why did Kerry feel "lonely" during her visit to China?

options.A: Because the dance studio in China was not professional.
options.B: Because she was unable to communicate with her family there.
options.C: Because she was separated from her dance partner Francis.
options.D: Because the training in China was too difficult for her.

questions.explanation: 文章在提到Kerry在中国的经历时说“I was in China for six weeks... but I felt lonely”，紧接着Francis描述他们分开的时间为“horrible”，说明孤独感源于两人无法共同练习。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the future schedule of the two dancers?

options.A: They plan to stay in the UK for the rest of the winter.
options.B: They have cancelled all their future competitions due to weather.
options.C: They are scheduled to travel to Italy and China for professional engagements.
options.D: They will quit ballroom dancing to focus on their academic studies.

questions.explanation: 文章结尾明确指出“The pair head to Italy in January for a competition, before jetting off to China to appear on a TV show”，说明接下来的安排包括前往意大利参加比赛和去中国参加电视节目。

papers.body_text: Magazine Articles Why Are Some Women Training for Pregnancy Like It’s a Marathon? He Leaked the Secrets of a Southeast Asian Scam Compound. Then Things Got Truly Uncivilized I Let AI Agents Plan My Vacation—and It Wasn't Terrible My Couples Retreat With 3 AI Chatbots and the Humans Who Love Them How to Beat Jet Lag

questions.stem: The article titles suggest that which of the following is a recurring theme in recent discussions?

options.A: The ethical implications of gene editing and AI development.
options.B: The economic impact of renewable energy in developing nations.
options.C: The challenges of international relations between the US and China.
options.D: The growing influence of social media on political discourse.

questions.explanation: A significant portion of the article titles, such as 'He Went to Prison for Gene-Editing Babies. Now He’s Planning to Do It Again,' and multiple titles related to AI ('Your First Humanoid Robot Coworker Will Probably Be Chinese,' 'China’s AI Boyfriend Business Is Taking On a Life of Its Own,' 'OpenAI's Fidji Simo Plans to Make ChatGPT Way More Useful—and Have You Pay For It,' 'All of My Employees Are AI Agents, and So Are My Executives,' 'Why AI Isn't Coming for Hollywood. It's Already Arrived,' 'I Let AI Agents Plan My Vacation—and It Wasn't Terrible,' 'My Couples Retreat With 3 AI Chatbots and the Humans Who Love Them'), point towards the ethical and developmental aspects of these emerging technologies.

questions.stem: Based on the provided titles, what can be inferred about the author's perspective on technological advancement, particularly AI?

options.A: Unreservedly optimistic about its potential to solve global problems.
options.B: Deeply concerned about its potential for misuse and unintended consequences.
options.C: Balanced, acknowledging both the promise and the perils of AI.
options.D: Skeptical about its long-term viability and impact.

questions.explanation: While many titles highlight the rapid advancements and new applications of AI (e.g., 'China’s AI Boyfriend Business,' 'AI Isn’t Coming for Hollywood,' 'AI Agents Plan My Vacation'), others hint at negative aspects or complexities ('Why AI Breaks Bad,' 'The Future of AI Isn’t Just Slop,' 'He Also Gets Paid to Hate AI'). This suggests a nuanced view that explores both the positive and negative facets of AI's development and integration.

questions.stem: The diverse range of topics covered in the article titles implies that the publication aims to appeal to a readership interested in:

options.A: Niche scientific research and historical analysis.
options.B: Current events, technology, and societal trends.

questions.explanation: The titles span a broad spectrum of contemporary issues, including cutting-edge technology (AI, robotics, gene editing, EV batteries), global politics and international relations (US-China, ICE), societal trends (wellness, dating, parenting), and even unique human interest stories. This breadth indicates an interest in covering relevant and evolving aspects of modern life and technology.

questions.stem: Which of the following topics, judging by the titles, is presented with a sense of urgency or potential crisis?

options.C: The renewable energy sector in China.
options.D: The adoption of humanoid robots in the workplace.

questions.explanation: The title 'China’s Renewable Energy Revolution Is a Huge Mess That Might Save the World' directly conveys a sense of significant challenge ('Huge Mess') intertwined with a potentially world-altering outcome ('Might Save the World'), suggesting an urgent and critical situation.

questions.stem: The inclusion of topics like 'The Story of DOGE, as Told by Federal Workers' and 'One Vigilante, 22 Cell Tower Fires, and a World of Conspiracies' suggests an interest in:

options.A: Exploring the intersection of finance, internet culture, and conspiracy theories.
options.B: Analyzing the formal structures of government agencies.
options.C: Documenting the history of emerging technologies.
options.D: Promoting scientific breakthroughs in cellular biology.

questions.explanation: These titles touch upon unconventional subjects: DOGE relates to cryptocurrency and internet phenomena, while the cell tower fires and conspiracies delve into fringe beliefs and unusual events. This combination points to an interest in examining how modern phenomena, including online culture and mistrust, intersect with economic and social realities.
